description: Detailed physical and acoustic properties of each cymatics material. Includes density, viscosity (for fluids), particle size, surface tension, and frequency response characteristics relevant to cymatic pattern formation.

materials.name: Sand (Fine grain, SiO₂)
materials.notes_for_visualization: Optimal particle size for general Chladni demonstrations. The classic material. Yields clear nodal lines with moderate amplitude and frequency. Best sieved to 100-150 μm for sharpest patterns.

materials.name: Sand (Coarse grain, SiO₂)
materials.note_per_material: Deep bass — suited to low frequencies only
materials.notes_for_visualization: Requires higher amplitude. Best for very large plates at low frequencies. Produces coarser patterns with thick nodal ridges.

materials.name: Lycopodium Powder (Lycopodium clavatum spores)
materials.grain_shape: Highly spherical, surface with fine reticulate texture
materials.pattern_lifetime_after_vibration_stops: Fragile — any air current destroys it
materials.note_per_material: Full range — responds across entire audible spectrum
materials.notes_for_visualization: Most sensitive dry material. Required for high-frequency patterns above 2 kHz. Responds to near-field radiation pressure in addition to direct plate contact. FIRE HAZARD — do not use near flames. Hydrophobic coating prevents humidity clumping.

materials.name: Sodium Chloride (Table Salt, NaCl)
materials.hygroscopicity: High — absorbs moisture rapidly above 75% RH
materials.electrical_conductivity: Insulating when dry; conductive when moist
materials.pattern_lifetime_after_vibration_stops: Permanent until disturbed or humidity-induced clumping
materials.color_description: Brilliant white, cubic crystals sparkle under directional light
materials.note_per_material: Crisp white tone — maximum visual contrast against metal plates
materials.notes_for_visualization: Best visual contrast of all dry materials. Cubic crystal facets produce sparkle effects. AVOID on steel plates for extended periods — NaCl causes corrosion. Use on aluminum, glass, or acrylic. Requires dry conditions.

materials.name: Water (H₂O, distilled)
materials.color_description: Clear — appears blue-gray under appropriate lighting
materials.note_per_material: A (fundamental) — responds to all frequencies as Faraday waves
materials.notes_for_visualization: Faraday waves appear at HALF the driving frequency. Use 3-8 mm water depth. Distilled water essential for reproducibility — tap water surface tension varies. Temperature matters: 20°C standard. A small drop of isopropyl alcohol dramatically changes pattern by lowering surface tension.

materials.name: Cornstarch Suspension (Non-Newtonian Oobleck, ~60% w/v)
materials.electrical_conductivity: Low (insulating when dry, slightly conductive when wet)
materials.pattern_lifetime_after_vibration_stops: Collapses to flat surface within 1 second
materials.notes_for_visualization: Mix ratio critical: 1.5 parts cornstarch by mass per 1 part water. Suspension settles — stir before each use. Produces dramatic 3D behavior unlike any other material. Collapses instantly when vibration stops. Most visually dramatic cymatics material. Clean immediately — hardens when dry.

materials.name: Iron Filings (Fe)
materials.pattern_lifetime_after_vibration_stops: Semi-permanent — magnetically attracted to steel plate (use non-magnetic plates)
materials.notes_for_visualization: Use ONLY on non-magnetic plates (aluminum, glass, acrylic). Iron filings are attracted to steel plates and will not sort properly. Can be combined with a nearby magnet to create hybrid acoustic-magnetic pattern. Hard to clean completely — use disposable plate surface.

materials.name: Graphite Powder (C, crystalline)
materials.color_description: Dark gray with metallic sheen, nearly black
materials.note_per_material: Full range mid-to-high — excellent high-frequency sensitivity
materials.notes_for_visualization: Very fine particles make it nearly as sensitive as lycopodium but with dark color for contrast against light-colored plates. Platelet shape creates interesting textures — grains partially orient in the acoustic field. Electrically conductive nodal lines can be used as resistors or capacitors for experimental electronics. Stains everything — use gloves.

materials.name: Wheat Flour (all-purpose, ~75 μm)
materials.grain_shape: Irregular starch granules and protein aggregates
materials.notes_for_visualization: Excellent accessible material for home and classroom experiments. Similar behavior to salt but produces softer-edged patterns due to irregular particle shapes. Dry before use in humid conditions. Do not leave on metal plates long-term — natural oils in flour can leave stains.

materials.name: Caster Sugar (sucrose, ~200 μm)
materials.pattern_lifetime_after_vibration_stops: Fragile — moisture can dissolve and fuse crystals
materials.color_description: White to near-transparent; sparkles under directional lighting
materials.notes_for_visualization: Distinctive sparkle under studio lighting due to crystal transparency and faceting. Confectioner's (powdered) sugar provides finer particles for higher-frequency work. Extremely moisture-sensitive — work quickly in humid environments. Attracts insects — do not leave set up.

materials.name: Colored Craft Sand (dyed SiO₂)
materials.grain_shape: Sub-rounded to rounded (same as natural sand)
materials.color_description: Available in many colors; each batch behaves identically to natural sand
materials.note_per_material: Full spectrum — full audio range, artistic color application
materials.multi_frequency_technique: Sequential technique: apply red sand, excite at frequency A and photograph. Add blue sand on top, excite at frequency B and photograph. Two frequencies visible as two colors simultaneously in composite image.
materials.notes_for_visualization: Identical acoustic properties to natural sand. The dye coating does not significantly affect particle density or friction. Use for artistic multi-frequency documentation, color-coded educational displays, and commercial/art installations.
